coredata.dc:title: Effect of foliar application of Siraitia grosvenorii-derived carbon dots on growth, photosynthesis, and physiological characteristics of Siraitia grosvenorii

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel nanomaterial, exhibit significant potential across the entire crop lifecycle, including enhanced photosynthesis, improved stress resistance, and increased crop biomass production. Moreover, crop waste can serve as a precursor for CDs, thereby creating a sustainable, circular approach to agricultural practices. In this study, we propose a sustainable solution for managing wasted fruits generated during the frozen storage, processing, and cultivation of Siraitia grosvenorii while simultaneously promoting seedling growth. Wasted S. grosvenorii fruits were precursors to synthesize S. grosvenorii-derived CDs (L-CDs) via hydration and thermal treatment. Five different L-CDs concentrations (0, 2, 2.5, 4, and 10 mg·mL−1) were tested as foliar fertilizers on S. grosvenorii seedlings. Among these, the 4 mg·mL−1 concentration demonstrated optimal growth-promoting effects. In terms of photosynthetic enhancement, the 4 mg·mL−1 treatment resulted in notable increases in several key parameters: total chlorophyll content (14.46 %), photosynthetic rate (47.69 %), stomatal conductance (104.76 %), intercellular carbon dioxide concentration (22.54 %), transpiration rate (139.62 %), ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase enzyme activity (26.94 %), and carbohydrate content (40.67 %). From a physiological perspective, the 4 mg·mL−1 concentration significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes, including peroxidase (58.28 %), superoxide dismutase (108.30 %), and catalase (1.73 %), while increasing the osmoregulatory proline (31.98 %) content. These effects helped maintain the balance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and mitigated oxidative stress, as evidenced by reductions in malondialdehyde (45.48 %) and improved ROS scavenging efficiency. This study highlights the potential of using S. grosvenorii fruit waste to produce effective foliar fertilizers, presenting a sustainable and efficient strategy for enhancing S. grosvenorii agricultural production.

(a) Full-range XPS spectra of the L-CDs. (b) The deconvoluted C(1s) XPS spectra fitted peaks (using Gaussian function) for L-CDs: C=C (284.1 eV), C−C (284.7 eV), C−N (285.5 eV), C−O (286.1 eV), and C=O (287.8 eV). (c) N(1s) spectra and fitted peaks of XPS: amino N (399.0 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), quaternary N (400.1 eV), and amide N (O=C−N, 401.1 eV). (d) O(1s) spectra and fitted peaks of XPS: C=O (530.9 eV), C−O (531.5 eV), and C−O−C (532.4 eV).

UV–Vis-NIR (a) and PL excitation and emission (b) spectra of L-CDs in aqueous solution. (c) PL emission spectra of l-CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 370 to 460 nm. Insets in (a) show the associated photographs taken under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation (right).

Growth indicators under different L-CDs concentrations. (a) Plant height, (b) shoot fresh and dry weights, (c) and root fresh and dry weights. Values are presented as mean ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate the significance based on Duncan's post hoc analysis at p < 0.05 and p < 0.01.

Effect of different L-CDs concentrations on the leaf growth characteristics of Siraitia grosvenorii seedlings. (a) Images of S. grosvenorii seedling leaves, (b) leaf area, (c) leaf length and width, and (d) leaf fresh and dry weights. Values are the means ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate the significance based on Duncan's post hoc analysis at p < 0.05 and p < 0.01, respectively.

Effects of different L-CDs concentrations on the (a) net photosynthetic rate, (b) stomatal conductance, (c) intercellular CO2 concentration, (d) transpiration rate, (e) photosynthetic pigment contents, and (f) RuBisCO activity and carbohydrate content. Values are the means ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate the significance based on Duncan's post hoc analysis at p < 0.05 and p < 0.01.

Effects of different L-CDs concentrations on the (a) POD activity and proline content, (b) SOD activity, (c) CAT activity, and (d) MDA content. Values are the means ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters indicate the significance based on Duncan's post hoc analysis at p < 0.05 and p < 0.01.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel nanomaterial, exhibit significant potential across the entire crop lifecycle, including enhanced photosynthesis, improved stress resistance, and increased crop biomass production. Moreover, crop waste can serve as a precursor for CDs, thereby creating a sustainable, circular approach to agricultural practices. In this study, we propose a sustainable solution for managing wasted fruits generated during the frozen storage, processing, and cultivation of Siraitia grosvenorii while simultaneously promoting seedling growth. Wasted S. grosvenorii fruits were precursors to synthesize S. grosvenorii-derived CDs (L-CDs) via hydration and thermal treatment. Five different L-CDs concentrations (0, 2, 2.5, 4, and 10 mg·mL−1) were tested as foliar fertilizers on S. grosvenorii seedlings. Among these, the 4 mg·mL−1 concentration demonstrated optimal growth-promoting effects. In terms of photosynthetic enhancement, the 4 mg·mL−1 treatment resulted in notable increases in several key parameters: total chlorophyll content (14.46 %), photosynthetic rate (47.69 %), stomatal conductance (104.76 %), intercellular carbon dioxide concentration (22.54 %), transpiration rate (139.62 %), ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase enzyme activity (26.94 %), and carbohydrate content (40.67 %). From a physiological perspective, the 4 mg·mL−1 concentration significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes, including peroxidase (58.28 %), superoxide dismutase (108.30 %), and catalase (1.73 %), while increasing the osmoregulatory proline (31.98 %) content. These effects helped maintain the balance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and mitigated oxidative stress, as evidenced by reductions in malondialdehyde (45.48 %) and improved ROS scavenging efficiency. This study highlights the potential of using S. grosvenorii fruit waste to produce effective foliar fertilizers, presenting a sustainable and efficient strategy for enhancing S. grosvenorii agricultural production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Siraitia grosvenorii (Swingle) C. Jeffrey ex A. M. Lu & Zhi Y. Zhang, commonly known as monk fruit or Luo Han Guo, is a plant of the Siraitia genus in Cucurbitaceae. It is an important economic and medicinal plant in China with >300 years of medicinal history (Gong et al., 2019). The medicinal value of S. grosvenorii lies in its fruit, with mogroside V being the primary bioactive constituent. Mogroside V exhibits a broad spectrum of pharmacological effects, including anti-diabetic, anti-tumor, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neuroprotective, and anti-lipogenic properties, among others (Li et al., 2022). It is a non-toxic, low-calorie, sugar-free natural sweetener approved for use in health foods for obese and diabetic patients (Pawar et al., 2013). With the global focus on health and fitness, the demand for natural, high-performance, and low- or zero-calorie sweeteners in the food and beverage industry has surpassed that of traditional dietary sweeteners (Marco et al., 2023; Rizas et al., 2023). As the second largest source of natural sweeteners after Stevia, the global S. grosvenorii market is projected to achieve a steady compound annual growth rate of 4.37 % from 2019 to 2026, indicating a highly promising market outlook (Shivani et al., 2021). The cultivation area of S. grosvenorii is constantly expanding to meet market demand. However, the development of the S. grosvenorii industry has been affected by problems related to its cultivation. This species is not widely or commercially cultivated owing to its poor adaptability, unique developmental characteristics of dioecious plants, and high cultivation costs. S. grosvenorii generally grows in mountainous and hilly areas of southern China with an average annual temperature of 16–20 °C and an average annual precipitation of 1500–2002 mm, accounting for >85 % of the global production (Shivani et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2023; Zeng et al., 2011). There is no evidence of the domestication or cultivation of this species in countries other than China (Chomicki et al., 2020; Shivani et al., 2021). As a dioecious plant, S. grosvenorii is extremely difficult to pollinate naturally and requires artificial pollination to ensure production, which increases cultivation labor costs (Tu et al., 2017). Furthermore, the short life of flowers and high pollen viability is affected by abnormal weather, leading to low flowering and non-meeting between males and females during peak summer periods (Jia et al., 2011; Li et al., 2004; Tu et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018a,b). The current production response involves planting seedlings earlier and applying large amounts of fertilizer to promote seedling growth, achieving the successive differentiation of flower buds and early flowering (Jiang et al., 2008; Qin et al., 2019; Qin, 2007). S. grosvenorii requires a high demand for fertilizers to support its growth and production. According to standard production protocols, each plant requires a base application of approximately 5–10 kg·plant−1. Additionally, S. grosvenorii seedlings require approximately 3.4–3.95 kg·plant−1 of supplementary fertilizer during their growth cycle. These nutrient requirements are predominantly fulfilled through the combined use of farmyard manure and chemical fertilizers (Bai et al., 2008; Jiang et al., 2008, 2007). High fertilizer input increases the planting cost of S. grosvenorii. Chemical fertilizers are increasingly used to ensure agricultural production (Dadaşoğlu et al., 2023; Yağanoğlu and Aydin, 2024). They cause serious environmental pollution unfavorable for industrial development(Liu et al., 2011). However, current research on S. grosvenorii has focused on its chemical composition and pharmacological effects, while its cultivation could benefit from further research. The cropping pattern of using large amounts of fertilizer at the seedling stage to encourage growth and avoid high temperatures during flowering has not been effectively improved. Moreover, S. grosvenorii fruits typically require hot air or low-temperature drying before further use. Owing to process limitations, fruit waste is inevitably generated during production. Waste that cannot be repurposed or provide added value is often discarded directly, leading to substantial resource underutilization (Hong et al., 2022). Our long-term practical investigation showed that moldy fruits deteriorated during frozen storage. Using these wasted fruits comprehensively to accelerate the growth of S. grosvenorii seedlings and realize the virtuous cycle of the industrial economy is a key issue that needs to be solved urgently. Fortunately, carbon dot (CDs) nanotechnology provides an innovative and environmentally friendly approach to addressing these issues.

CDs, which are emerging nanomaterials with a general particle size of <10 nm that can be made from many types of carbon sources, have great application potential for the entire life cycle of crops (Li et al., 2021a). CDs are primarily involved in pre-harvest and post-harvest processes. Pre-harvest processes promote seed germination, nano-fertilization, and plant growth regulation, whereas post-harvest processes promote anti-bacterial preservation of harvested fruits and intelligent anti-counterfeit packaging. At the end of their life cycle, crops become agricultural waste, a valuable biomass that can be used to prepare CDs and re-enter the crop life cycle, thereby creating a closed loop (Guo et al., 2022a). Natural crop waste, including plant leaf extracts and discarded fruit and vegetable peels, is rich in phytochemical constituents and is used to synthesize high-value CDs as biomass materials that are green and environmentally friendly (Guo et al., 2022a,b; Omran et al., 2021). For example, rice husk, bagasse, and wheat straw serve as green and readily available precursors to produce fluorescent silicon nanomaterials (Bose et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2016). Moreover, CDs derived from biomass favor the regulation of plant growth, photosynthesis, and physiological processes (Guo et al., 2022a,b; Li et al., 2020b). This proves that CDs can promote plant growth by improving photosynthetic activity (Guirguis et al., 2023). Foliar application of biomass-derived CDs synthesized from soybeans, walnuts, and dried laver can promote Brassica chinensis growth via enhanced photosynthesis (Cheng et al., 2023). Similarly, plant-derived CDs synthesized from Piper betle leaves increase the chlorophyll content of strawberries and enhance their growth and yield (Belal et al., 2023). CDs have been widely studied in the field of abiotic stress (extreme temperatures, droughts, floods, high salinity, and heavy metal pollution). Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived CDs can effectively alleviate oxidative damage in Italian lettuce under salt stress and relieve heat stress by enhancing antioxidant enzyme activity and osmotic regulation (Li et al., 2021b; Wang et al., 2021b). Proline-functionalized CDs (Pro-CDs) mitigate salinity stress in grapevines by enhancing both enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant defenses, including increased levels of phenolics and anthocyanins (Gohari et al., 2021). Therefore, the main objectives of our study were to (1) prepare S. grosvenorii-driven CDs (L-CDs) using a hydration and heating method as a green and environmentally friendly solution for processing fruit waste of S. grosvenorii and (2) study the effects of L-CDs as foliar fertilizers on seedling growth, photosynthesis, and physiological characteristics of S. grosvenorii seedlings.

S. grosvenorii fruit waste (2 g) and urea (0.5 g) were placed in a mortar, fully ground, and then placed into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, to which 20 mL of ultrapure water was added, and then sealed. After heating at 250 °C for 6 h and cooling to 25 °C, the L-CDs were filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous membrane and stored at 4 °C. The particle size and surface structure of the L-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Their surface functional groups and structure of the L-CDs were characterized using Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and their optical properties were characterized using UV and fluorescence spectrophotometry.

We used the S. grosvenorii seedling variety “Qing-pi,” produced by Guilin GFS Monk Fruit Corporation Co., Ltd. Guilin, China. The experiment was conducted in April 2023 in the experimental greenhouse of the Guangxi Institute of Botany, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (25.08°N, 110.30°E), using the same cultivation and management model. The experiment utilized L-CDs as foliar fertilizers, applied at four concentrations: 2, 2.5, 4, and 10 mg·mL−1. The leaves of the plants were evenly sprayed once a week for four consecutive weeks. Plants were sprayed with water as the control (CK, 0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of L-CDs on the growth and biomass of S. grosvenorii
ce:section.ce:para: After four weeks of experimental treatment, 10 plants from each group were randomly selected for measurement. Plant height was measured, and two intact functional leaves at the same position were collected per plant. One leaf was used to measure length, width, area, and fresh and dry weight, while the other one was put into a liquid nitrogen tank and immediately stored at −80 °C to determine physiological index contents. The plants in each treatment group were harvested, and their shoot and root weights were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of L-CDs on the photosynthesis of S. grosvenorii
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthetic pigments were determined using the 95 % ethanol method. Photosynthetic parameters were measured using a portable photosynthesis measurement system (Li-6400XT, LI-COR, Lincoln, NE, USA) to determine the leaf net photosynthetic rate (Pn), stomatal conductance (Gs), intercellular carbon dioxide concentration (Ci), and transpiration rate (Tr) (Wang et al., 2010). Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (RuBisCO) activity, a key enzyme in photosynthesis, was determined using a Wuhan Gene Beauty Biotechnology Co., Ltd. ELISA assay kit (Wuhan, China). The carbohydrate content was determined using the sulfuric sulfate-anthone method (Morris, 1948).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of L-CDs on the physiological characteristics of S. grosvenorii
ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidant enzyme activity and lipid peroxidation were assessed by determining superoxide dismutase (SOD) and peroxidase (POD) enzyme activities using a Solarbio (Beijing, China) biochemical kit (Xu et al., 2023), catalase (CAT) activity using the Wuhan Gene Beauty Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China) ELISA kit, malondialdehyde (MDA) levels using the thiobarbituric acid method (Yang et al., 2023), and proline content using the ninhydrin method (Bates et al., 1973).
Data were analyzed using SPSS 26.0 with a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation and plotted using Origin 26.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure, and composition characterization of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Characterizations were performed to investigate the morphology, surface functional groups, structure, and synthesized L-CDs composition. The high-resolution TEM image in the inset of Fig. 1a shows that the L-CDs had a crystal structure with a lattice distance of 0.23 nm, consistent with the (100) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon. As shown in Fig. 1b, the L-CDs were well-dispersed quasi-spherical particles with a narrow size distribution (3.45–6.55 nm). These results suggest that the L-CDs consisted of nanodots with a uniform size distribution.

Raman spectroscopy, FT-IR, and XPS were used to investigate the surface structure and elemental composition of the L-CDs. First, Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate the hybridization state of the carbon materials in the prepared L-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the Raman spectra of the L-CDs had two obvious peaks, the disordered band (D) at 1380 cm−1 and the crystalline band (G) at 1585 cm−1, which corresponded to the sp3 and sp2 hybridization orbitals in the carbon materials, respectively. The coexistence of the D and G bands in carbon materials is an important indicator of their defect density of carbon materials. The ID/IG ratio of the L-CDs prepared in this study was ∼0.69, indicating their well-graphitized structure. The synthesized L-CDs were characterized using FT-IR to further explore surface functional groups. The peak at 1113 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the C−O bond, the spike at 1600 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=O, the peak at 1400 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the C−N bond, the peak at 3202 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of O−H, and the peak at 3402 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the N−H bond (Fig. 1d). The results showed that the L-CDs had hydrophilic groups, such as −COOH and −OH, on the surface and good water solubility. XPS was used to analyze the surface states and elemental compositions of L-CDs. The XPS spectra showed three characteristic peaks at 284.9 eV, 399.5 eV, and 531.6 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2a ). In the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 2b), the signal peaks of C1s appeared at 287.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 285.5 eV, 284.7 eV, and 284.1 eV, which were assigned to C=O, C−O, C−N, C−C and C=C, respectively. This indicated that the L-CDs were mainly composed of graphitic carbon (sp2) and disordered carbon (sp3), and their surfaces were rich in hydroxyl and hydrophilic groups, such as carbonyl/carboxylic acid groups. The high-resolution spectra of the N1s were fitted to obtain four Gaussian peaks (Fig. 2c), which corresponded to amino-type N (399.0 eV), pyrrole-type N (399.5 eV), quaternary ammonium-type N (400.1 eV), and amide-type N (401.1 eV). The XPS spectra proved that the L-CDs contained N and hydrophilic groups, such as amino groups. High-resolution spectra of O1s showed three peaks at 530.9 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.4 eV, which were attributed to the C=O, C−O, and C−O−C characteristic groups, respectively (Fig. 2d). The results of the XPS analysis indicated that the surface of the L-CDs might be rich in oxygenated and hydrophilic groups, such as carboxyl and amino groups, which could improve the dispersion and stability of the L-CDs in water.

The aqueous solutions of the L-CDs appeared slightly yellow under fluorescent light and emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (inset of Fig. 3a ). The UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of L-CDs were characterized to investigate their optical properties. The UV–visible absorption spectra of L-CDs showed a strong characteristic absorption peak at 282 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C, and a shoulder peak at 334 nm, which was attributed to the n-π* transition of C=O and the defective surface states formed by nitrogen-containing structures (Fig. 3a). In the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 3b), the optimal excitation peak of L-CDs was λex = 370 nm and the optimal emission peak was λem = 442 nm. Using quinine sulfate as a reference (Quantum yield (QY) = 54.0 % in 0.1 M H2SO4), the fluorescence QY of L-CDs in an aqueous solution was 10.6 %, comparable to that of previously reported CDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, when L-CDs were excited at different excitation wavelengths (370–460 nm), the fluorescence emission peaks of L-CDs shifted to longer wavelengths (442–506 nm) with increasing excitation wavelengths, indicating the excitation wavelength dependence of the L-CDs. This excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence variation was likely related to the particle size inhomogeneity and the excessive number of emission defect sites on the carbon core surface. This remarkable fluorescence emission suggested that the L-CDs can act as light sources for plant photosynthesis, improve photosynthetic efficiency, and promote plant growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different concentrations of L-CDs on S. grosvenorii
Effects of different concentrations of L-CDs on the growth of S. grosvenorii seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Spraying S. grosvenorii with different L-CDs concentrations significantly affected the plant height and seedling biomass. The optimal concentration to promote S. grosvenorii seedling growth was 4 mg·mL−1 (Fig. 4 ). Compared with CK, the L-CDs treatment groups showed increases in plant height (Fig. 4a) of 11.05–49.22 %, shoot fresh and dry weights (Fig. 4b) of 30.97–47.97 % and 6.05–137.01 %, respectively, and root fresh and dry weights (Fig. 4c) of 32.17–54.20 % and 25.76–53.03 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different concentrations of L-CDs on the photosynthetic characteristics of S. grosvenorii seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The L-CDs significantly enhanced the photosynthetic characteristics of S. grosvenorii seedlings, including leaf growth, photosynthetic pigment content, photosynthetic rate, RuBisCO activity, and carbohydrate accumulation. Fig. 5a and b show that different concentrations of L-CDs promoted the leaf growth characteristics of S. grosvenorii seedlings. Compared with CK, leaves sprayed with L-CDs were greener, wider, and stronger. However, the growth-promotion effect was initially strong but weakened with increasing concentrations. The application of 4 mg·mL−1 L-CDs had the most significant boosting effect, with a 61.85 % increase in leaf area, a 74.41 % increase in the fresh weight of leaves, and a 66.59 % increase in the dry weight of leaves, compared with CK (Fig. 5c and d). The application of all L-CDs significantly increased the photosynthetic parameters of S. grosvenorii seedlings (Fig. 6a –d), and Pn was positively correlated with Gs, Tr, and Ci in the concentration range of 0 to 4 mg·mL−1. L-CDs at the optimum growth concentration of 4 mg·mL−1 resulted in the maximum Pn (47.69 %), Ci (22.54 %), and Tr (139.62 %) values. Results show that 2–10 mg·mL−1 L-CDs increased the chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and total chlorophyll content by 16.55–18.71 %, 7.47–13.17 %, and 11.96–14.46 %, respectively (Fig. 6e). The key photosynthetic enzyme, RuBisCO, exhibited a dose-dependent increase in activity ranging from 24.53 % to 32.10 % at a concentration of 2–10 mg·mL−1 compared to the CK (Fig. 6f). Different concentrations of L-CDs had different effects on the carbohydrate content of photosynthetic products (Fig. 6f). The concentration with 2 mg·mL−1 showed no significant difference compared to the CK group. However, concentrations with 2.5, 4, and 10 mg·mL−1 significantly increased carbohydrate content by 14.98 %, 40.67 %, and 44.94 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different concentrations of L-CDs on antioxidant enzyme activity and the osmotic regulator proline in S. grosvenorii seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs activate the plant antioxidant enzyme system and increase the content of the osmotic regulator, proline. Different concentrations of L-CDs had different effects on the antioxidant enzyme activity of S. grosvenorii seedlings. The POD enzyme activity and proline content of S. grosvenorii seedlings showed a tendency to increase and then decrease and peaked at 4 mg·mL−1, which was 58.28 % and 31.98 % higher than that of CK, respectively (Fig. 7a ). SOD activity increased significantly by 108.30 % at 4 mg·mL−1 (Fig. 7b). L-CDs treatment groups enhanced CAT activity by 1.71–3.18 % compared to CK (Fig. 7c). The MDA content accumulated at levels of 4.36–63.55 % with increasing L-CDs concentrations (Fig. 7d).

Previous studies have demonstrated that CDs positively affect crop growth (Li et al., 2020b). For example, Agnol et al. exposed lentil sprouts to spirulina-derived CDs and found that CDs at 0.1 mg·mL−1 improved the root length, seedling length, and biomass by 48 %, 105 %, and 120 %, respectively, compared to the control (Agnol et al., 2021). Ji et al. indicated that the foliar application of 5 mg·L−1 of CDs improves the fresh and dry weights of soybean shoots and roots by 17.4 %, 31.5 %, 8.7 %, and 10.6 %, respectively (Ji et al., 2023). S. grosvenorii seedlings were selected as the research model to examine L-CDs effects on their growth and evaluate L-CDs effectiveness as a novel foliar fertilizer in enhancing plant growth rates. Our results showed that the growth of S. grosvenorii exhibited a dose-dependent response to L-CDs within the concentration range of 0–4 mg·mL−1, with a threshold at 4 mg·mL−1, beyond which the growth-promoting effect diminished (Fig. 4). Compared to the CK group, plant height (Fig. 4a) increased by 11.05–49.22 %, shoot fresh and dry weights (Fig. 4b) increased by 30.97–47.97 % and 6.05–137.01 %, respectively, and root fresh and dry weights (Fig. 4c) increased by 32.17–54.20 % and 25.76–53.03 %, respectively, in the L-CDs treatment group. These findings indicate that L-CDs can promote plant growth and have the potential to become novel foliar fertilizers. Our findings align with those of other studies (Ji et al., 2023) (Tan et al., 2021), demonstrating that the foliar application of CDs significantly promoted the growth of soybeans, rice, and corn.

Photosynthesis, which is divided into light and dark reactions, is crucial for plant growth. The light reaction absorbs and converts energy under light, whereas the dark reaction is chemical and catalyzed by several enzymes and can be carried out in the presence or absence of light. Studies have shown that CDs improve plant growth by facilitating photosynthesis (Hussain et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021a). Photosynthesis in the aerial parts of S. grosvenorii seedlings was evaluated to further explore the mechanism by which L-CDs enhance plant growth. Given the complexity of photosynthesis, our discussion focuses on several key factors, including leaf physiological and morphological development, Pn, Gs, Ci, Tr, chlorophyll content, and RuBisCO enzyme activity. The leaf, a vital organ for photosynthesis, significantly influences the light interception capacity of a crop, thereby influencing plant biomass and resource utilization (Weraduwage et al., 2015). Simultaneously, a linear relationship between leaf and plant mass has been observed during the trophic stage of plant growth (Weraduwage et al., 2015). Therefore, the physiological and morphological development of the leaves was determined first. Our findings indicated that the L-CDs treatment promoted the growth of S. grosvenorii leaves (Fig. 5a–d). Plant height (Fig. 4a), shoot dry weight (Fig. 4b), fresh and dry root weights (Fig. 4c), and leaf characteristics (Fig. 5b–d) exhibited initially increasing and then decreasing trends with increasing L-CDs concentration. The 4 mg·mL−1 L-CDs treatment had the most significant enhancement effect, with a 61.85 % increase in leaf area, a 74.41 % increase in leaf fresh weight, and a 66.59 % increase in leaf dry weight compared with CK. The results reported by Tan et al. reveal that cucumber seedling leaves treated with CDs have a promoting effect on physiological morphology; under different CDs concentrations, the leaf area also increased, but the efficiency was relatively low (Tan et al., 2022). Similarly, the results reported by Belal et al. revealed that strawberry plant leaves exposed to CDs visibly increased compared with those treated with water (Belal et al., 2023). Subsequently, important parameters for measuring photosynthesis, such as Pn, Ci, Gs, Tr, chlorophyll content, RuBisCO activity, and carbohydrates of the largest functional leaves in S. grosvenorii seedlings, were analyzed. Pn, Tr, and Ci exhibited an overall trend of initially increasing and then decreasing after the L-CDs treatment (Fig. 6a–d). At the optimum growth concentration of 4 mg·mL−1, L-CDs maximized the Pn (47.69 %), Ci (22.54 %), and Tr (139.62 %) concentrations compared to CK. Gs showed a dose-dependent increase in activity, ranging from 9.52 % to 109.52 % at concentrations of 2–10 mg·mL−1, compared to the CK group. Studies have shown that higher Gs can lead to faster Tr and higher Pn (Santiago et al., 2004; Tan et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2020). This aligns with the results of our experiment within the concentration range of 0–4 mg·mL−1. However, at a concentration of 10 mg·mL−1, Gs was not correlated with Tr or Pn. Crop yield is closely related to CO2 assimilation during photosynthesis (Zelitch, 1982). Therefore, we presumed that this was related to the reduced 10 mg·mL−1 concentration of Ci. Chlorophyll is an important pigment for photosynthesis, providing biochemical energy for carbon sequestration reactions by harvesting photons. Higher chlorophyll content increases light absorption, favoring Pn (Belal et al., 2023). Consistent with previous research (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a), our study demonstrated that L-CDs can significantly promote the production of photosynthetic pigments. Results show that 2.5–10 mg·mL−1 of L-CDs increased the total chlorophyll content by 11.96–14.46 % compared to the CK group (Fig. 6e). Plant photosynthesis fixes carbon dioxide and further assimilates it into a carbon source for growth and development through the action of the key enzyme RuBisCO (Parry et al., 2013). Increasing RuBisCO enzyme activity increases the rate of carbon assimilation and enhances photosynthesis (Perdomo et al., 2017). Fig. 6f shows that RuBisCO exhibited a dose-dependent increase in activity ranging from 24.53 % to 32.10 % at concentrations of 2–10 mg·mL−1, compared to the CK group. Carbohydrates are the main products of photosynthesis and provide the materials and energy for plant development (Hartmann et al., 2018). Fig. 6f shows that carbohydrate levels at 2.5 mg·mL−1, 4 mg·mL−1, and 10 mg·mL−1 increased significantly by 14.98 %, 40.67 %, and 44.94 %, respectively, compared to the CK group. Consistent with previous studies (Hao et al., 2023; Li et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018), these findings demonstrate that enhanced photosynthesis leads to carbohydrate accumulation, which subsequently promotes plant growth.

Studies have shown that CDs can activate plant antioxidant enzyme systems and increase the content of the osmotic regulator proline (Li et al., 2020a; Su et al., 2018). A similar result was observed in the present study. POD activity and proline content in S. grosvenorii seedlings followed a trend of increasing and then decreasing, peaking at a concentration of 4 mg·mL−1, where they were 58.28 % and 31.98 % higher than the CK group, respectively (Fig. 7a). Similarly, Fig. 7b shows that SOD activity significantly increased by 108.30 % at 4 mg·mL−1. L-CDs treatment groups also exhibited significant differences from CK in CAT activity, which increased by 1.71–3.18 %. However, as shown in Fig. 7d, the MDA content increased with increasing concentrations of L-CDs, indicating that excessive L-CDs induced oxidative damage in S. grosvenorii seedlings. This may be attributed to reactive oxygen species (ROS) production during photosynthesis, consistent with previous results of increased light-induced damage under treatment with core-CDs (Chen et al., 2016; Swift et al., 2021).

CDs can conjugate with chloroplasts and transfer energy to the latter, thereby accelerating the electron transport rate in photosystem II (PSII) (Chandra et al., 2014; Kou et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020c; Wang et al., 2021c). Apart from abiotic stress, ROS is produced by electrons generated by excess light reactions and limited CO2 assimilation capacity (Gururani et al., 2015; Khorobrykh et al., 2020). Plants use various endogenous strategies, including oxidative defense systems and osmolyte accumulation, to maintain the balance between ROS production and clearance to overcome ROS stress (You and Chan, 2015; Zhao et al., 2022; Zulfiqar and Ashraf, 2023). Thus, the antioxidant defense system of S. grosvenorii seedlings enhanced POD, SOD, and CAT enzyme activities and elevated proline content to mitigate L-CDs-mediated oxidative damage, which is consistent with the results observed in this study (Chen et al., 2016; Kara et al., 2023; Li et al., 2020a; Su et al., 2018). In addition, proline, a beneficial solute in plants that accumulate under stress and non-stress conditions, is a potential source of nitrogen and carbon and plays an important role in plant flowering and development (Ghosh et al., 2022; Khorobrykh et al., 2015). In the present study, proline followed the same trend as that of plant height and aboveground fresh weight growth, with previous findings showing that proline promotes wheat plant height and increases wheat branch fresh weight under non-stress conditions (Ambreen et al., 2021). Previous studies have shown that CDs increase the relative expression of psbA, PsbP, and PsbQ to promote PSII system activation responses (Wang et al., 2021a,c). A stronger PSII may reflect the ability of these leaves to make key metabolic adjustments, including increased proline accumulation and Pn to maintain higher antioxidant protection and a better balance between light capture and energy use (Ambreen et al., 2021; Sperdouli and Moustakas, 2012; Zulfiqar and Ashraf, 2023), consistent with the proline accumulation and Pn trends in this study.

L-CDs showed favorable effects on the growth and physiology of S. grosvenorii at the seedling stage; however, long-term observation of the entire reproductive period is still needed. In future studies, we will conduct research on the flowering and fruiting stages to provide a theoretical basis for improving the added value of S. grosvenorii waste and realizing waste recycling.

In summary, L-CDs synthesized from fruit waste of S. grosvenorii positively influenced the growth, photosynthetic properties, antioxidant enzyme activities, and proline content of S. grosvenorii seedlings. The L-CDs showed excellent performance in the growth of S. grosvenorii seedlings when the concentration increased to 4 mg·mL−1. L-CDs significantly promoted the development of leaf growth characteristics and photosynthetic pigment synthesis in the photosynthetic organs of S. grosvenorii seedlings. They also improved photosynthetic parameters and increased the activity of the key photosynthetic enzyme RuBisCO to increase carbohydrate accumulation in photosynthetic products, thus promoting the growth of S. grosvenorii seedlings. In addition, S. grosvenorii seedling growth was significantly promoted by increasing the capacity of the plant protection system, including the enhancement of antioxidant enzymes (POD, SOD, and CAT) and an increase in proline content, which maintains the balance between ROS production and scavenging. Even when considering the increase in MDA content, the effect of L-CDs was significantly better than that in the case of no application. These results suggest that L-CDs have excellent potential for future agricultural applications.

Yumei Zheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lizhen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xinmei Qin: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hui Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yuan Yuan: Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Mengying Wu: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Rongchang Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiyang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
